questions.question: What was Rama Natha's unusual obsession? Why did he neglect his land?
How did people exploit Rama Natha's belief in the magic potion?
Why was Madhumati worried about her husband?
What task did sage Mahipati give Rama Natha? Why did he ask him to do everything himself?
What happened when Rama Natha sprinkled the dew on a copper vessel? Why was he angry?
What was the sage's real lesson for Rama Natha? Why did he play a trick instead of telling the truth directly?
Write the meanings of these words from the story: potion, tended, muttered, precious, wisdom, chant.
Write sentences using these pairs of homophones: I/eye, your/you're, dew/due, son/sun, one/won, ate/eight, see/sea.
Match the phrases using connecting words (because, before, as soon as, as): The children submitted their work / the bell rang.
What does hard work mean to you? Give three reasons why you think hard work is important.
Work in pairs to describe a banana using the five senses — taste, colour, smell, touch, and when you like to eat it.
What did Mr Raven hold in his beak? What did the Fox want?
How did Reynard the Fox flatter the Raven? Why did he call him 'Sir'?
Why did the Raven open his beak? What happened when he did?
What is the moral of the poem? Why does Reynard say that pride is 'rather unwise'?
Imagine someone praises you too much. How would you react?
Match these words with their meanings: perched, morsel, seek, pride, eyed, limb, woods.
Write the rhyming words from each stanza of the poem.
Choose the correct opposites from the box: perched, forgot, foolish, pride, laughed, ignore, glow.
Find examples of alliteration in the poem. What is alliteration?
Narrate the poem in the form of a story, giving a different ending.
Write the story as a conversation between Reynard and Mr Raven.
Why was Gajaraj unhappy despite living in comfort in the royal stables?
How did Gajaraj and Buntee first meet? How did their friendship develop?
What happened when the farmer took Buntee away? How did both friends react?
Why did the farmer decide to let Buntee go back to Gajaraj?
What was 'unlikely' about the friendship of Gajaraj and Buntee?
Fill the crossword with opposites of words from the story: kind, laughed, found, finished, happy (add prefix un-, dis-, mis-).
Put these verbs from the story into the correct tense column: Simple Past, Past Progressive, Past Perfect.
Complete sentences using 'neither...nor' and 'either...or'.
Describe how your friendship with your best friend started. Use words like same, different, both, like, unlike.
Talk to your friend and find out your common likes and dislikes. Share your findings with the class.
What does the speaker value most in life according to the poem?
How does the poet feel about having special friends? What word describes this feeling?
What does the poet want to use her 'heart' to see? Why not just her eyes?
Why is this poem called 'A Friend's Prayer'? Give one reason.
What does 'make no judgements from afar' mean? Why is this important in friendship?
Select words similar in meaning to 'blessed' from: excited, grateful, understood, thankful, relaxed.
List qualities of a good friend using these clues: he__ful, lo__l, k__d, c__ing, ho__t.
Fill in the blanks: My friend is always ___ to me. She is very ___. He always ___ me.
Write three sentences about three of your friends — why you like each one of them.
What would you pray for your friend? First speak about it and then write it down.
Who is 'Neem Baba'? Why does the old man ask Amber to call him that?
Where was the neem tree originally born? Where can it be found now?
How is the neem tree useful to farmers? Mention at least three ways.
Which parts of the neem tree are used to make medicines?
Why is the story of the neem tree's name called 'thought-provoking'?
Identify compound words from the text: something, neem oil, well known, childhood, seed powder, thought-provoking. Sort them into open, closed, and hyphenated.
Match present tense verbs with their past forms: become/became, find/found, make/made, discover/discovered, tell/told, give/gave.
Amber speaks to Neem Baba as if speaking to a person. This figure of speech is called personification. Give two more examples.
Write a paragraph about a tree in your neighbourhood — where it is, how it looks, and why you like it.
Collect dry leaves from four different trees. Make a card with the leaf shape and speak about the process to your class.
What was the bird's first home? What did it think the world was made of?
How did the bird's understanding of the world change at each stage?
Why did the bird call itself 'very blind' after it left the nest?
What does the bird conclude about the world at the end of the poem?
Who do you think are the bird's 'neighbours'? Why don't they know how the world is made either?
Replace the underlined words with their correct meanings: pale, fluttered, finally, hard work (labour).
Write the rhyming words from each stanza: well/shell, other/mother, find/blind, made/neighbours.
Use describing words (pointed, green, thin, brown, small, wooden, hanging, round) to write six sentences about a bird's nest.
How would the world look to a baby? Write five sentences describing the world from a baby's perspective.
Which bird would you like to be? Speak about it — what you look like, where you live, and what message you have for humans.
What was Prabhat's attitude toward winning and losing at the beginning of the story?
How did Prabhat cheat in the badminton match against Surya?
How did Surya's attitude toward losing differ from Prabhat's?
Why could Prabhat not sleep well the night after winning the match?
How did Prabhat change by the end of the story? Why is the title 'Change of Heart' suitable?
Why do you think sporting spirit is important?
Write the meanings of: defeat, on the contrary, terrible, sensed, significant, conducted, phenomenon, hopeless.
Circle the verbs and sort them as past perfect (first/old action) or past progressive (second/new action).
Fill in the blanks with the past perfect form of the verbs: Prabhat ___ (finish) his homework before going to the match.
Imagine you are Prabhat. Write a diary entry describing your feelings on the night after the match.
Use the magic words 'please', 'thank you', and 'sorry' in role-play situations with a partner.
Where were the children playing? What game were they playing?
Why couldn't the speaker catch her breath?
What does 'When blue dark turns to black' mean?
Who is 'The Winner' in the poem? Why does 'Night win'?
Why do the trees seem to 'creep close' in the evening?
Write the meanings of: next to the creek, I can't even catch my breath, when blue dark turns to black, trees creep close.
The poet uses personification — 'Night wins!' Find another example of personification in the poem.
Name different water bodies (creek, river, lake, etc.) and create a word web.
Write a paragraph about your playtime — what time you play, who you play with, what games, and when you come home.
Enact a situation where a child comes home late after play. The parent is upset, the child explains.
What is the 'Ek Bharat, Shreshtha Bharat' programme about?
What is Aipan? Which state does it come from and what materials are used?
Describe the process of making Dhokra metal craft. How old is this art form?
What is common between coconut shell craft from Kerala and Kondapalli toys from Andhra Pradesh?
Why does Akanksha's family make Aipan? When do they make it?
Write the meanings of: geru, carve, scrap, stable, biodegradable, tamarind, sawdust, dyes, enamel.
Use sequencing words (to begin, first, next, then, after that, finally, at last) to describe how you get ready for school.
Make five sentences using present tense with different pronouns (I, You, He, She, They) and verbs (ask, know, tell, draw, make, practise, speak).
Write a paragraph about an art form from your region — what it is called, what materials are needed, and how it is made.
Speak about a local art form of your region to your class. Include its name, materials, and how it is made or presented.
How does the poet describe the kites in the first two lines? What are they compared to?
Why does the child wish to be small and light as air?
What songs would the child hear while flying on the kite?
Why would the people below stand and stare?
If you were the kite carrying the child high up in the air, what would you tell them?
Name the parts of a kite: nose, spine, cover, kite line, tail, reel. Use them in a conversation.
Find examples of simile ('like coloured birds', 'light as air') and alliteration ('wild wind', 'stand and stare') in the poem.
Underline the rhyming words in each stanza: fly/sky, air/there, wings/sings, down/town, stare/air.
Write a cinquain poem about a kite using the pattern: noun, two adjectives, three verbs, two adverbs, one synonym.
Imagine yourself as a kite. Write a paragraph of about 100 words with the title 'I am a Kite'.
Share a kite-flying experience with your classmates — where, when, why, and how.
